topic: What we rebuilt in Recap Studio
oneSentenceAnswer: We made Recap Studio's pages into one file you can double-click offline, made it work in any editor, and told the truth about its score.
fiveMinutePath: See the one problem that made every page un-shareable.
Meet the new piece that fixes it: a self-contained HTML template.
Learn why it now works in editors other than Claude Code.
Understand the honesty fix around the quality score.
Check what's left and what to try next.
whyItMatters: Before this session, a generated page only worked behind a web server — double-clicking the file showed a broken, unstyled mess. That defeats the whole point of "here's a page you can share".
The tool claimed it was reviewed by seven AI reviewers, but the score was actually a quick rule-based check. Saying so plainly protects the project's credibility.
Most people don't live in one editor. Making it work in Cursor, VS Code, and the terminal — not just Claude Code — is the difference between a demo and a tool people rely on.

keyIdeas.title: One file, no server, works offline
keyIdeas.body: A new renderer writes the whole page — styling included — into a single HTML file with no JavaScript. You can email it, open it on a plane, or double-click it. No build server required.

keyIdeas.title: A template other tools can borrow
keyIdeas.body: The look-and-feel is now a shared package, not locked inside one app. Any sibling tool that can describe its content can get the same calm, dark, mobile page for free.

keyIdeas.title: Runs in any editor
keyIdeas.body: A small command-line tool and a fixed plug-in interface mean the page generator and checker work in Cursor, VS Code, the terminal, and more — not only inside Claude Code.

keyIdeas.title: Honest about the score
keyIdeas.body: The built-in score is a fast rule-based check (does every claim cite a source? is it short enough?). It does not read the sources or run an AI. We now say that everywhere it appears.

keyIdeas.title: Safer out of the box
keyIdeas.body: The settings file shipped with deploy and email switched on. We reset it to off, kept it off the public repo, and left a clearly-marked example to copy.

diagrams.alt: Before: content went through a web-app builder and produced files with broken links that only worked on a server. After: the same content also goes through a new renderer that produces one self-contained HTML file you can double-click.

timeline.title: Audited the whole tool
timeline.body: Eleven specialist passes mapped what worked and what didn't, with file-level evidence.

timeline.title: Built the self-contained renderer
timeline.body: A new package turns content into one offline HTML file. Proven by a screenshot at phone and desktop sizes.

timeline.title: Opened it to other editors
timeline.body: A command-line tool and a fixed plug-in interface make it work outside Claude Code.

timeline.title: Fixed honesty and safety
timeline.body: Reframed the score, reset risky defaults, and hardened the validator against bad input.

examples.title: Make a shareable page in one command
examples.body: Run `recap render content.json -o page.html`. You get a single file you can email; the person opens it with a double-click, no install.

examples.title: The demo proves it
examples.body: `pnpm render:demo` produced a 47 KB page with a hand-drawn diagram, scored sections, and cited sources — verified visually on a phone-width screen.

analogies.setup: Think of the old output like a website that only loads on the office network.
analogies.takeaway: The new output is like a PDF: one file, opens anywhere, looks the same for everyone.

misconceptions.myth: A 9.7/10 means seven AI reviewers approved the page.
misconceptions.truth: It's a fast rule-based score (citations present, page short enough, no secrets). The AI review only runs inside Claude Code.

misconceptions.myth: The old web app was thrown away.
misconceptions.truth: It stays — for hosting on Vercel. We added a second path that makes one shareable file.

glossary.definition: A web page whose styling and content are all inside one file, so it works with no server and no internet.
A standard way for editors to talk to small tools. Fixing our messages to follow it is what lets other editors use Recap Studio.
A command you type in a terminal. Ours, `recap`, renders or scores a page anywhere.
Saved example data used so the demo runs offline with no live research.

practicalTakeaways: Generate a recap, then just double-click the HTML — no server needed.
Use `recap render` / `recap validate` from any editor's terminal.
Trust the score as a quick structural check, not as a fact-check — run /recap in Claude Code for the real review.
Copy recap-studio.config.example.ts and turn on deploy/email only when you want them.

sourceMap.title: packages/html-renderer — the self-contained renderer
sourceMap.summary: renderToHtml() builds one offline HTML file with inlined CSS and zero JS.

sourceMap.title: scripts/render-html.mjs — pipeline wiring
sourceMap.summary: Reads content JSON and writes artifacts/<slug>/recap-<slug>.html.

sourceMap.title: packages/cli — the recap command
sourceMap.summary: recap render|validate, works without Claude Code.

sourceMap.title: packages/mcp-server — render tool + MCP-compliant transport
sourceMap.summary: Adds render_recap_html and fixes responses to MCP's text content format.

sourceMap.title: scripts/validate.mjs — deterministic checks
sourceMap.summary: Rule-based scoring; now guards bad input and is described honestly.

sourceMap.title: recap-studio.config.example.ts — safe defaults
sourceMap.summary: Deploy and email off by default; real config is gitignored.

sourceMap.title: docs/audit — the 11-pass audit
sourceMap.summary: Evidence-backed findings that drove every change this session.

uncertaintyNotes: Reading time is estimated from word count, not measured.
The before/after diagram is a simplified picture of the build, not the literal code path.
